coredata.dc:description: Identifying single or groups of animals has significantly advanced our understanding of animal biology and ecology. However, labelling is extremely difficult in small animals, like soil invertebrates. Due to the complexity of current methods, the dynamics and interactions of these populations are often studied indirectly. Labelling nematodes or microarthropods such as collembolans or soil acari has been challenging due to the high cost, potential toxicity, genetic modification requirements, cellular processes interference, and photobleaching. In this scenario, no methods can be applied to large numbers of microorganisms at once due to their mentioned practical and biological limitations and cost. In this work we show that quantum carbon dots (Cdots) are effective for labelling infective juveniles (IJs) of entomopathogenic nematodes (EPNs). In in vitro assays the IJs gradually acquired fluorescence, as Cdots accumulated in the lysosome-related organelles from their intestine cells, peaking at day 14, and with no lethal or sub-lethal effects on IJs. Fluorescence was clearly distinguishable from the natural auto-fluorescence of non-labelled IJs and persisted for months in IJs transferred to water. We and non-experts easily differentiated between similar species of EPNs and between two strains of S. feltiae placed in the same matrix (soil or water). We demonstrated for the first time the feasibility of labelling large numbers of IJs (hundreds of thousands/millions) with Cdots at minimal cost without any adverse effects for over a year. Our findings could be the starting point for detailed and large-scale field investigations on nematodes and other small organisms, allowing deeper understanding of their roles in soil ecosystems. This method provides a cost-effective and reliable tool for advancing research in the ecology of soil invertebrates, such as the interactions occurring in communities or between specific organisms, movement and dispersal, population dynamics or ecosystem services in a cryptic environment difficult to study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Lethal and sub-lethal effect of two types of carbon quantum dots on infective juveniles of Steinernema feltiae. A) Percentage of mortality (mean ± s.d.) of S. feltiae strain (UOH-010) exposed to EDA-Cdots and S. feltiae strain (UOH-011) exposed to U-Cdots at different concentrations during one week. B) Number of nematodes (Median ± Q1-Q3) capable of penetrating G. mellonella larvae. C) Number of offspring produced per cadaver of Galleria mellonella larva in White traps.

Dynamic of the acquisition of Cdots by Steinernema feltiae (left panel) represented as arbitrary units of fluorescence (mean ± s.d.). Retention of Cdots of Steinernema feltiae (right panel) after washing the nematodes in water represented as arbitrary units of fluorescence (mean ± s.d.). All the measured points revealed significant differences between labelled nematodes and control (asterisk).

Infective juveniles of Steinernema feltiae labelled with Cdots A) Live and dead nematodes showing accumulation of Cdots (green U-Cdots and cyan EDA-Cdots) with a magnification of 100X under UV-ligth. Notice the diffusion of the colour on dead nematodes compared to live ones. Bars represent 100 μm. B) Colocalization of Cdots and LysotrackerTM deep red in the lysosome-related organelles with a magnification of 1.000X under UV-ligth (Cdots) and green filter (LysotrackerTM). Bars represent 25 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Microscope images of an extraction of infective juveniles (IJs) of Steinernema feltiae (strain UOH-011) labelled with U-Cdots placed in soil samples for 8 weeks showing soil particles, and other nematodes at 100X Bars represent 100 μm. A) Visible light, B) UV-light and C) A sketch illustrating soil particles (white) and seven nematodes (grey), with three identifiable as infective juveniles (EPNs) D) Identification error and time of observation (mean ± s.d.) of two entomopathogenic nematode species labelled with U-Cdots (Steinernema feltiae) or EDA-Cdots (Steinernema unicornum) in the same glass slide. The asterisks represent significant differences in the percentage of error.

head.ce:title: Where are my nematodes? labelling and visualising entomopathogenic nematodes in vivo using carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Identifying single or groups of animals has significantly advanced our understanding of animal biology and ecology. However, labelling is extremely difficult in small animals, like soil invertebrates. Due to the complexity of current methods, the dynamics and interactions of these populations are often studied indirectly. Labelling nematodes or microarthropods such as collembolans or soil acari has been challenging due to the high cost, potential toxicity, genetic modification requirements, cellular processes interference, and photobleaching. In this scenario, no methods can be applied to large numbers of microorganisms at once due to their mentioned practical and biological limitations and cost. In this work we show that quantum carbon dots (Cdots) are effective for labelling infective juveniles (IJs) of entomopathogenic nematodes (EPNs). In in vitro assays the IJs gradually acquired fluorescence, as Cdots accumulated in the lysosome-related organelles from their intestine cells, peaking at day 14, and with no lethal or sub-lethal effects on IJs. Fluorescence was clearly distinguishable from the natural auto-fluorescence of non-labelled IJs and persisted for months in IJs transferred to water. We and non-experts easily differentiated between similar species of EPNs and between two strains of S. feltiae placed in the same matrix (soil or water). We demonstrated for the first time the feasibility of labelling large numbers of IJs (hundreds of thousands/millions) with Cdots at minimal cost without any adverse effects for over a year. Our findings could be the starting point for detailed and large-scale field investigations on nematodes and other small organisms, allowing deeper understanding of their roles in soil ecosystems. This method provides a cost-effective and reliable tool for advancing research in the ecology of soil invertebrates, such as the interactions occurring in communities or between specific organisms, movement and dispersal, population dynamics or ecosystem services in a cryptic environment difficult to study.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: A major challenge in understanding the biology and ecology of animals is identifying an individual or a group of individuals unequivocally. For vertebrate animals the solution is quite simple and many marking devices/techniques have been developed, such as rings, tattoos, photos or cuts; also, more sophisticated systems include chips, transmitter collars or satellite tags (Dugger, 2007). However, as these animals become smaller and simpler, labelling becomes more complex and fewer techniques are available. In the case of invertebrates (insects, ticks, crustaceans, snails, etc.) several methods for individually marking larger insects include using ink spots, engraving codes onto exoskeleton, or individual tags(Bell et al., 2003; Piper, 2003; White et al., 2020). These methods produce permanent marks but they are often costly, time-consuming, and the number of individuals finally marked is rather limited. To overcome the latter issue, fluorescence dusts (Hagler and Jackson, 2001), stable isotopes (Mainali et al., 2022) and genetic markers (Raphael et al., 2014) for labelling and tracking thousands of insects such as sterile fruit flies has been extensively used, but some problems related to their elevated costs, need of highly skilled staffs, toxicity in high application rates, emergence of adults, reproduction rates, lack of persistence of the pigments, among others, often occur (Dominiak et al., 2023; Hagler and Jackson, 2001). But what about much smaller organisms that are difficult to see with the naked eye, such as nematodes, tardigrades, collembolans or soil mites. Is it possible to develop a cost-effective, scalable method for labelling cohorts of individuals with a shared marker in large numbers of animals such as thousands or hundreds of thousands?

Generally the dynamics and interactions of microarthropods are often studied indirectly (Maaß et al., 2015; Pollierer and Scheu, 2021) due to technical limitations. The current methods for labelling micro- and meso-organisms (ranging from microscopic to millimetre-sized) have been challenging for a long time and have involved various techniques depending on the type of organism, such as fluorescent proteins (Tsien, 1998), chemical dyes (like DAPI, FITC, Rhodamine, etc)(Chazotte, 2011), radio and stable isotopes (Gawne et al., 2022) among others. These techniques are expensive, potentially toxic depending on the material, require genetic modification to express fluorescent proteins (Greer III and Szalay (2002)) or can interfere with cellular processes (Ansari et al., 2016), and in many cases photobleaching (photochemical fading) occurs (Shaner et al., 2005) and none can be applied to large numbers of organisms at once.

Since their discovery in the 1980 s (Brus, 1983; Ekimov et al., 1985) numerous attempts have been made to use quantum dots as biomarkers. However, once their toxicity was demonstrated due to their heavy metal core composition (i.e. Se, Cd, etc)(Feliu et al., 2016; Liang et al., 2022), other non-toxic alternatives have been developed such as carbon quantum dots (Cdots) (Sun et al., 2006). These carbon-based nanomaterials offer significant advantages, including excellent biocompatibility, high water solubility, and, in many cases, cost-effective and simple synthesis methods. In recent years, huge efforts have been made to use Cdots for biomedical applications in vitro in order to develop diagnosis and therapy protocols. However, so far Cdots have mostly been tested to prove non-toxicity in a handful of multicellular organisms including mice, zebra fish, and the nematode Caenorhabidits elegans Maupas, (Rhabditida: Rahbditidae) or just to suggest their “potential” to become biomarkers (Cong et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020; Singh et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2018).

Entomopathogenic nematodes (EPNs) are small soil organisms capable of killing insects through an association with symbiotic bacteria that produce an acute septicaemia. The only stage capable of surviving in the soil, searching and finally invading the insect host is a special form of the third juvenile stage called infective juvenile (IJ)(San-Blas et al., 2024). The IJ is a nonfeeding stage which is developmentally arrested (diapause stage) until finding a proper host. Due to their insect specificity, rapidness of action (24–96 h), ease of reproduction and field application, and total harmlessness to non-host organisms, EPNs have become one of the most popular biological pest control agents today (San-Blas, 2013). Despite their enormous potential, many of their biological and ecological aspects remain unknown due to technical limitations in their study. For example, EPNs are known to have a very variable behaviour, either among populations or among cohorts within the same population. Consequently, it is impossible to distinguish between individuals from different groups under a microscope when exposed to identical environments. Moreover, the complex interactions among EPN species pose significant challenges for accurate identification, as precise taxonomic expertise is required. Furthermore, the interactions between different EPN species are complex because differentiating between them requires precise taxonomical skills and the results could be biased and contain a high level of uncertainty. Therefore, the current infeasibility of labelling EPNs represents a challenge and linger questions regarding the fate of the nematodes applied in experimental studies. Under laboratory conditions, IJs can live for several months, however, once applied to the field more than half of them die within the first 24 h and only a fraction persists beyond a few days or weeks (Smits, 1996) due to a combination of abiotic and biotic factors (Blanco-Pérez et al., 2024; Hazir et al., 2024). In fact, it has been shown on many occasions that nematodes applied to soil are unable to establish themselves in the ecosystem (Smits, 1996). Although some individuals survive, their fate and role in these environments remain unknown due to the difficulty in distinguishing them from other nematodes (including native EPNs) in the soil. Therefore, the current infeasibility of labelling EPNs represents a challenge and linger questions regarding the fate of the nematodes applied in experimental studies.

Here we report the use and application of two types of Cdots for labelling IJs of Steinernema feltiae Filipjev, (Rhabditida: Steinernematidae) in vivo. In this work, we hypothesized that (1) the Cdots have no lethal or sublethal effects on IJs, (2) their penetration to the nematodés body occurs by diffusion and gradually, and (3) the labelled IJs can be extracted from soil samples and easily identified. This technology offers endless possibilities for designing new experiments, for improving the understanding of their biology and ecology or labelling commercial strains for field applications and, in addition, requires non-specialists to correctly identify the marked nematodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Cdots synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesise the urea Carbon dots (U-Cdots; 3–5 nm), 3 g of citric acid and 3 g of urea (both from Merck, Germany) were added to 10 mL of ultrapure water (MiliQ system), forming a transparent solution following Qu et al. (2012). The solution was heated in a 750 W household microwave oven for 5 min, during which time the solution turned to a dark brown solid. The resulting material was suspended in 50 mL falcon tubes with 20 mL of ultrapure water and centrifuged (at 3000 rcf for 20 min). The supernatant was transferred to a new falcon tube and centrifuged again (repeating this procedure three times). The collected resulting solution of Cdots was diluted to 50 mL by adding deionized water (stock solution). The fluorescence and green colour were confirmed using a UV transilluminator.

To synthesise the ethylenediamine carbon dots (EDA-Cdots; 2 nm), 0.21 g (1 mmol) of citric acid and 0.18 g (3 mmol) of ethylenediamine (both from Merck, Germany) were dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water (MiliQ system) and stirred forming a clear solution following Qu et al. (2014). The solution was heated in a 750 W household microwave oven for 5 min, during which time the solution turned to a light brown paste-like material. This material was totally dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water with no further cleaning (stock solution). The fluorescence and cyan colour were confirmed using a UV transilluminator. Samples of the initial Cdots synthesised for pilots experiments in 2019 were kept in the laboratory and have remained stable and fluorescent since then at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nematode cultures
ce:section.ce:para: Three native EPN isolates were used, corresponding to Steinernema feltiae strains UOH010 and UOH011 and Steinernema unicornum Egington, Tymo, France, Merino & Hunt, (Rhabditida: Steinernematidae) strain UOH001. Multiplication was carried out following the method described by Kaya and Stock (1997), where groups of five last-stage larvae of Galleria mellonella L. (Lepidoptera: Pyralidae) were infected in 10 cm wide Petri dishes with filter paper at the base. 100 JI per larva were applied in a mL of distilled water. The Petri dishes were sealed and taken to the culture chamber for seven days. Those G. mellonella cadavers that showed signs of nematode infection were transferred to White traps for another seven days. The new infective juveniles (IJs) were subsequently collected and stored in dark conditions, refrigerated (4 °C), in cellular culture bottles with a permeable lid to allow gas exchange until their use before one month.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity, sublethal and transgenerational effects of Cdots on Steinernema feltiae
Evaluation of acute toxicity of Cdots on infective juveniles of s. Feltiae
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment was set up in 24 multi-well plates, using one plate for each Cdot solution. A suspension of 1000 IJ of the species S. feltiae in 0.1 mL of water was used in each well. A diluted series of the U-Cdots with 6 concentrations (strain UOH-011) or EDA-Cdots (strain UOH-010) were added randomly in the wells. The concentrations were 100 %; 75 %; 50 %; 25 %; 10 %, Control in 0.5 mL of solution, which was added to the EPNs, resulting in a total of 18 treatments, distributed randomly in the multi-well plate with 4 replicates. Subsequently, a count of live and dead EPNs was done at 24, 48 and 72, 120 and 168 h.

To count nematodes, for each of the treatments and hours, 125 µL were extracted and placed on a grid plate under a microscope. To assess viability, the IJ were gently touched with an eyelash attached to a dissection needle to verify movement as response. The experiment was repeated five times with three replicas each. The percentage of live nematodes was calculated and angular transformed prior to a two-way ANOVA analysis (concentration vs. time).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the sublethal effect of Cdots on infective juveniles of s. Feltiae
ce:section.ce:para: Thirty last instar larvae of G. mellonella were placed in 24 multi-well plates (one larva per well) filled with sterile sand (180-µm sieve) moisturised at 15 % v/v. Then, 0.1 mL of water containing one of five different doses of nematodes (4, 10, 30, 50, 80 IJ) previously exposed for 168 h in a solution of 10 % of Cdots were applied in each well randomly. The plates were sealed with parafilm and left in the incubator at 20 °C in darkness and were checked daily to determine the death of the larvae. A dead larva from each treatment was dissected and the number of IJs that entered inside the larva was counted. Another dead larva from each treatment was moved to White traps, and incubated at 20 °C in darkness. All the IJs that came out of the cadaver were harvested to know the total number of the offspring. The experiment was repeated thrice. The number of offspring was used with no transformation, and both results analysed with a one-way ANOVA. The number of penetrating IJs was analysed by univariate PERMANOVA applied to evaluate the effect of treatment (Cdots and control) and the used dose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transgenerational effect of Cdots on infectivity and pathogenicity of nematodes offspring
ce:section.ce:para: The new IJ collected from the previous experiment were further placed in 24 multi-well plates (40 IJs per well) containing a G. mellonella larva (10 in total). The plates were sealed and placed in an incubator at 20 °C and mortality was recorded every day until completing 96 h. After one week, five insect cadavers were taken randomly and placed in White traps individually until the new IJ emerged. After completing the emergence, the total IJ per trap were counted under a stereo microscope. The remaining five larvae were dissected to assess the number of IJs capable of penetrating the larvae. The experiment was repeated thrice. The results of mortality were arcsine transformed prior to the analysis. The number of offspring was used with no transformation, and both results analysed with a one-way ANOVA. The number of penetrating IJs was analysed by Lavernés test for medians.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long term exposure effect of Cdots on S. feltiae infectivity and pathogenicity
ce:section.ce:para: 50 ml of 10 % v/v stock solution of the Cdots (U-Cdots, EDA-Cdots) and water as control were placed into five cellular culture flasks containing 2,000 IJ/ml, which were kept into the fridge for 12 months at 4 °C. The assay was repeated thrice in the same conditions as the previous experiment [in a 24 multi-well plate, 40 IJs per well containing a g. Mellonella larva (10 in total)]. At the 6th and 12th month of storage and the resulting data were analysed as before.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acquisition of Cdots by S. feltiae through time
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment was done in 24 multi-well plates. 450 μL of a suspension containing 1,500 IJs were added to each well and 50 μL of the Cdots stock solutions were added to the wells. 500 μL of the IJ suspension in water only was used as control. The plates were sealed with parafilm and placed in a humid chamber to minimise evaporation and kept in an incubator at 20 °C in darkness. In the days 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 9, 11, 14, 17, 24 and 32, five IJ per well were mounted in distilled water on a microscope slide (for dead nematode pictures) or in an anaesthetic solution of 10 mM chlorine azide (CAS 13973–88-1) drop (for alive nematode pictures). To kill the nematodes, the slides were placed for 10 s on a hot plate at 80 °C and then under the microscope. The experiment was repeated five times with 3 replicates each, the control was repeated four times with 3 replicates. The total number of pictures taken were n = 2,178. After the image analysis (see below) the results were analysed by two-way ANOVA (fluorescence vs time) using Cdots and controls as fixed variables and time as random.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Retention of Cdots in S. feltiae in water
ce:section.ce:para: After day 32 of the previous experiment, the remaining IJ in each well were taken out, placed in an Eppendorf tube and washed in tap water three times by centrifugation (at 1,000 rcf for 5 min). The washed nematodes were placed again in 24 well plates with 500 μL of tap water and kept in the same conditions as the previous experiment. After 15, 30, 45, 60, 80, 180 and 365 days, five IJ per treatment were used for image acquisition following the same procedure as before. The total number of pictures taken were n = 770. The statistical analysis was performed as the previous experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Retention of Cdots and localization of S. feltiae in soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: Twenty soil samples were taken from a dune in the Pichilemu coast of Chile (−34.379230, −71.996034). These samples were pooled, homogenised and separated into two lots. The first lot (15 subsamples) was used to evaluate the natural presence of entomopathogenic nematodes using G. mellonella traps. After seven days, all the traps were checked for larva cadavers to confirm absence of EPNs in the samples. The second lot was divided into 15 subsamples which were placed in a 500 mL plastic pot each. For a week prior to the experiment, IJs of S. feltiae were soaked in one of the two Cdots solutions (10 %v/v). Before adding labelled nematodes to the potted soil, 25 IJs from each treatment (U-Cdots, EDA-Cdots and controls) were placed dead under the microscope (following the same protocol as before) for image acquisition (with the same setting explained in the next section). Then 2,000 IJs were placed into the pots (five pots per treatment) and kept in an incubator at 20 °C in darkness. After 1, 2, 4 and 8 weeks, a sample of 20 mL of soil was taken from each pot for nematode extraction using the sugar flotation technique. Then, 5 labelled IJs per sample were chosen randomly and used for image acquisition. The statistical analysis was performed as the previous experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colocalization of Cdots and LysotrackerTM in IJ lysosomes
ce:section.ce:para: Infective juveniles (several thousands) of S. feltiae were immersed in 50 mL of 10 % v/v stock solution U-Cdots or EDA-Cdots in cellular flask in a fridge at 4 °C. After seven days, a mL of the content of each flask containing the IJs was placed in an Eppendorf tube and washed in tap water three times by centrifugation (at 1,000 rcf for 5 min). The washed nematodes (pelletized in the bottom of the tube) were placed in a 0.25 mL Eppendorf tube. 100 μL of 10 mM LysotrackerTM Deep Red were added to the IJs and incubated for another week at 30 °C. After incubation, the IJs were extracted and placed in a glass slide under a stereoscope. With the help of an eyelash attached to a dissection needle, the IJs were picked up and transferred to a new slide containing a drop of water. Then, the slide was placed 10 s on a hot plate at 80 °C and then under the microscope for observation and analysis (section 2.9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practicality and easiness of observing labelled IJs, we conducted a study involving two groups of observers: one consisted of highly experienced nematologists and the other of undergraduate students or university staff with basic microscopy training. Both groups were asked to identify IJs from two similarly sized species (S. feltiae labelled with U-Cdots and S. unicornum labelled with EDA-Cdots) placed on the same slide. The main difference between both species is the presence of a tooth-like cephalic papilla and a shorter tail in S. unicornum compared to S. feltiae. After showing the differential characteristics of both species, the observers were challenged to identify the individuals from each group. First, they used the microscope with visible light, followed by one observation using UV light to classify the nematodes by their colour (green or cyan). The expert group (3 observers) performed 31 rounds of observations in total whereas the non-expert group (5 observers) performed 17 rounds of observations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Image acquisition and analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The IJs from all experiments were observed under an epifluorescence microscope (Leica 2500). In the case of the nematodes labelled with both Cdots, we used an UV filter (excitation filter = BP340-380; dichromatic mirror = 400; suppression filter = LP420); and in the case of the nematodes labelled in LysotrackerTM Deep Red, we used a TX2 green filter (excitation filter = BP560/40; dichromatic mirror = 595; suppression filter = BP645/75). The pictures were taken with a digital camera (Leica Flexacam1) using the software Leica Application Suite X. The parameters for photo acquisition were as follows: exposure time 56 ms, gain 6 and saturation of 80 %.

The images were analysed using the software ImageJ, the original pictures were transformed into 16-bits images. Fluorescence was measured in the anterior part of the intestine of the IJ selecting them manually with no further transformation. The RGB composition was measured by crossing the anterior part of the intestine transversally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results
Lethal, sublethal and transgenerational effects of Cdots on infective juveniles
ce:section.ce:para: After incubating Steinernema feltiae IJs in different concentrations of U-Cdots and EDA-Cdots for one week (acute toxicity), no differences in nematode mortality were observed compared to controls incubated in water (Fig. 1 A; S.Table 1) (F(5,120) = 1.93; p = 0.10; n = 630; α = 0.05 for EDA-Cdots) and (F(5,120) = 1.79; p = 0.12; n = 630; α = 0.05 for U-Cdots). Likewise, the number of IJs that entered the hosts (G. mellonella larvae) was similar in all treatments (U-Cdots = 49 ± 5 IJ/larva, EDA-Cdots = 48 ± 8 IJ/larva and control = 37 ± 1 IJ/larva) (F(2, 4) = 1.05; p = 0.37; n = 45; α = 0.05)(Fig, 1B). During infestation, host penetration involves search, localization and recognition by the nematode, therefore, we can surmise that these processes are not affected by labelling the IJ with any of the Cdots used. In addition, the amount of offspring produced by the nematodes, i.e. the number of new IJs emerged, once established in the cadaver of the hosts did not vary among any of the treatments (U-Cdots = 123*103 IJ/larva, EDA-Cdots = 123*103 IJ/larva and control = 150*103 IJ/larva) by one way ANOVA (F(2, 42) = 0.24; p = 0.79; n = 45; α = 0.05) (Fig. 1C). The production of new IJs depends on the reproductive capacity of the nematodes that colonise and establish themselves in the host cadaver. As we mentioned before, the nematodes are associated with symbiotic bacteria that have to overcome the insect's immune system, produce a septicaemia and become the source of food to support the nematodés development. The equal number of IJs capable of penetrating the larvae and the offspring production suggests firstly, that their reproductive capacity is not affected by the use of Cdots, and secondly, that the bacteria viability and functions would not be impeded. The EPN offspring produced in all treatments were equally capable of infecting and reproducing again in a new set of G. mellonella larvae (Supplementary Fig. 1,2). To evaluate the long-term effect of Cdots, we permanently maintained IJs in solutions of both Cdots for 12 months. These nematodes were assessed through the same infection assays at the 6th and 12th month and no differences in the number of IJs that penetrated the hosts and the offspring production compared to IJs which never were in contact with Cdots were observed (Supplementary Figs. 3-6). Therefore, these experiments demonstrated that the Cdots used are non-toxic to IJs and have no sublethal effects on S. feltiae at least within the measured parameters in any of the tested concentrations either in the short or long term of exposure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acquisition of Cdots by Steinernema feltiae through time
ce:section.ce:para: The IJs were placed in 10 % v/v C-dot solutions for up to a month and after 24 h differences in fluorescence were observed (F(2,393) = 8.87; p < 0.001; α = 0.05) (S.Table 2). The IJs gradually acquired the Cdots over time with a peak at 17.5 ± 8.3 and 23.2 ± 11.3 AU on day 14 for U-Cdots, and EDA-Cdots, respectively (F(10,2145) = 30.85; p < 0.0001; n = 2178; α = 0.05). The IJs labelled with U-Cdots exhibited 189 % more fluorescence than the control nematodes, while those labelled with EDA-Cdots showed 283 % more fluorescence when observed through a UV filter. Apart from those differences in fluorescence, the labelled IJs showed large differences in colour and amount of fluorescence compared to the control IJs (Fig. 2 , Supplementary Fig. 7A and Supplementary Video 1). The level of auto-fluorescence of the IJs in the control treatment varied slightly throughout the experiment ranging from 4.08 ± 2.7 to 6.45 ± 2.4 AU (Coburn et al., 2013) but it was not enough to be confused with the IJs labelled with the Cdots. Stained nematodes were readily distinguishable under the microscope, both in live and dead (killed) specimens, based on these two parameters (Fig. 3 A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Retention of Cdots in S. feltiae in water
ce:section.ce:para: To assess whether the IJs retain the Cdots inside their bodies, the Cdot solutions were washed from the nematodes and replaced with tap water. The fluorescence dropped by 43 % in U-Cdots and 35 % in EDA-Cdots compared to the values before washing them, but remained clearly labelled and identifiable from the control IJs which exhibited a fluorescence of 4 to 6.44 AU (F(4, 530) = 11.9; p < 0.0001; n = 614; α = 0.05) (Fig. 2). This drop in the fluorescence could be the result of the washing process of a weak attachment of Cdots in nematodes tissues such as cuticle, epidermis, cytoplasmic contents or organelles other than lysosomes but more research needs to be done. Despite fluorescence dropped in the labelled nematodes it is worth mentioning that the whole experiment lasted more than a year (32 days of the IJs submerged in Cdots and 365 days after washing in water the Cdots from the IJs). The fluorescence of the U-Cdots varied between 120 d and 180 d (F(2,102) = 9.031p < 0.0001) but then remained with no further changes until the end of the experiment at 365 d (p = 0.89). The overall reduction of fluorescence of the nematodes labelled with U-Cdots throughout the whole experiment was 26.19 %. This fluorescence dropping was similar in the nematodes labelled with EDA-Cdots which had a difference between 120 d and 180 d (F(2,102) = 6.5p = 0.002) but did not change further at the end of the experiment p = 0.6. In this case the overall reduction from the beginning of the experiment to the end was 36.25 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Retention of Cdots and localization of S. feltiae in soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: In the case of the nematodes placed in soil samples, those labelled with U-Cdots did not show any loss of fluorescence with values between 25.2 and 30.6 AU (330 % more than the controls). Fluorescence in IJs labelled with EDA-Cdots slightly decreased from 22.8 to 16.9 AU (25 %) in the first two weeks of the experiment but remained stable up to the eighth week when the trial ended. Remarkably, fluorescence of the stained nematodes showed 208 % more intensity than those IJs from the controls. As in the previous experiments, the control nematodes presented a slight increase in the amount/level of auto-fluorescence throughout the experimental period (60 days), varying from 4.4 to 8.4 AU The fluorescence intensity exhibited by the nematodes labelled with both Cdots allowed us to identify them even below or among soil particles and other organisms, including other non-entomopathogenic nematode species (Fig. 4 A-C). It should be noted that the nematode extraction technique used included the addition of a concentrated sucrose solution (450 g sugar/L deionized water) for some minutes and loss of fluorescence was not detected after extraction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colocalization of Cdots and LysotrackerTM in IJs lysosomes
ce:section.ce:para: Both Cdots used were visibly highly concentrated in the lysosome-related organelles (also called gut granules) of the nematodes’ intestine cells (Clokey and Jacobson, 1986; Delevoye et al., 2019; Hermann et al., 2005; Kostich et al., 2000) and further confirmed by co-localization of the Cdots with the specific lysosome marker LysoTracker™ Red DND-99 by using a green filter (Fig. 3B and Supplementary Fig. 8). The IJs labelled with U-Cdots showed a distinctive green colour in their intestines (RGB as rMean = 0.41, gMean = 130.22, and bMean = 7.23) whereas those labelled with EDA-Cdots showed a cyan colour (RGB as rMean = 0.61, gMean = 130.46, and bMean = 224.13) both clearly different from the pale blue autofluorescence of the control IJs (RGB as rMean = 0.36, gMean = 28.34, and bMean = 39.68) (Supplementary Fig. 5B). After killing the nematodes for observation, a diffusion in both Cdots treatments (and controls) was observed over time. This effect resulted in the Cdots spreading throughout the entire body of the IJs (Fig. 3A). Interestingly, despite this spreading, the Cdots retained their original colours and fluorescence intensity observed within the intestinal lysosomes (Supplementary Fig. 7B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation
ce:section.ce:para: A clear advantage of this type of labelling is the possibility of using nematodes in vivo, recovering them and sorting them for later experiments since they do not lose the Cdots while they are still IJs. Besides, once the IJs have been labelled, they can be easily distinguished from one another, making it possible to combine all the nematodes in the same experiment and identify them very precisely. The results were dramatic; the experts were able to correctly identify 83.7 ± 9.9 % of the IJs in 318 ± 48 s under visible light and 95 ± 6.3 % of the IJs in 140 ± 48 s using UV light (t(51,38) = 4.14; p < 0.0001; n = 62; α = 0.05) and (t(54,66) = 8.95; p < 0.0001; n = 62; α = 0.05) for identification accuracy and time of observation respectively. In the case of the non-specialist, they correctly identified only 47.8 ± 8.1 % of the IJs, and the observation took 393 ± 79 s with visible light; however, when observing the fluorescence under UV light, the correct identification rose to 90 ± 6.3 % in just 251 ± 66 s (t(29,97) = 12.64; p < 0.0001; n = 34; α = 0.05) and (t(31,64) = 4.40; p < 0.0001; n = 34; α = 0.05)(Fig. 4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion
ce:section.ce:para: Nematodes are the most abundant organisms on earth. In a handful of soil, hundreds to thousands of individuals of different species can be found (van den Hoogen et al., 2019). The possibility of tracking these small organisms in this medium is crucial for ecological studies. However, current methods for this are based on morpho-phenological, molecular, and microscopic analysis that are time consuming and require special skills. In addition, most of these methods require that the nematodes are dead. Therefore, evaluating and discriminating how any organism behaves in a matrix as complex as the soil is tremendously difficult. The use of Cdots in these experiments proved no toxicity, no sublethal effects and stability for labelling the IJs of entomopathogenic nematodes beyond a year of experimentation. Also proved that labelling nematodes with both Cdots facilitated the unambiguous identification of nematodes under epifluorescence microscopy, even in the presence of background autofluorescence in unlabelled specimens. The nematodes’ autofluorescence is well known in many species (Babu, 1974; Forge and MacGuidwin, 1989; Komura et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021) and can change during their lifespan (Davis et al., 1982) but it is clear that the labelled IJs of this investigation have big differences in the intensity of fluorescence and the colour produced by the Cdots acquired by the nematodes. The mean amount of fluorescence was 3 to 4-fold (U-Cdots and EDA-Cdots respectively) the autofluorescence measured in the control nematodes. In the case of EDA-Cdots, some values were significantly higher (> 100 AU) than the controls. This is because these Cdots achieve quantum yields of 94 % due to their N-doping synthesis, which uses citric acid as a carbon source and ethylene diamine as a nitrogen source (Qu et al., 2014) whereas the U-Cdots reach 40 % of quantum yields (Qu et al., 2012).

In both cases, the nematodes gradually accumulated the Cdots, reaching peak fluorescence approximately two weeks after immersion. The Cdots were accumulated in the lysosomes like organelles in the same fashion as other Cdots in human (Liu et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018), rat (Liu et al., 2019), and zebrafish cells (Qin et al., 2020). This was demonstrated by using a red specific tracker probe to lysosomes (LysoTrackerTM) with the Cdots at the same time. That probe is highly selective lysosomes due to their low pH contents and penetrates easily through cell membranes in living cells. In fact, we speculate that both Cdots and LysoTrackerTM entered the nematode bodies by diffusion through the cuticle because the IJ is an arrested juvenile stage which does not feed and its digestive system is non-functional (San-Blas, 2013). In general, the accumulation of Cdots in experiments with C. elegans occurs in intestinal cells, similar to the pattern observed in our experiments; however, the nematodes used are L4 larvae and young adults fed with bacteria [Escherichia coli Migula, (Enterobacterales: Enterobactereaceae)] grown in an artificial media containing Cdots (Li et al., 2020).

The observed diffusion of both Cdots (and controls) sometime after the death of the IJs could be similar to a phenomenon observed in C. elegans known as death fluorescence (DF)(Coburn et al., 2013). The compound that produces a burst fluorescence in dead nematodes has been chemically identified as anthranilic acid glucosyl esters derived from tryptophan that spread upon the intestinal necrotic cells. In this case it is probable that the Cdots also spread after cellular necrosis, behaving in the same manner as DF and are contained inside the nematode cadaver. This could indicate that the fluorescence properties of the Cdots remain inside the nematode even after the necrotic cell death pathway of the intestinal cellular structures takes place (Coburn et al., 2013).

Entomopathogenic nematodes have proven to be very variable organisms due to the large biological and ecological differences (San-Blas et al., 2024) that can exist between IJs that emerge from the same insect cadaver (Rolston et al., 2006), populations of the same species (Campos-Herrera and Gutiérrez, 2014) and, obviously, different species (Alatorre-Rosas and Kaya, 1990). In our experiments, we were able to differentiate between two different populations of S. feltiae and between two different species (S. feltiae and S. unicornum) placed in the same matrix (soil or water). In addition, it was found that fluorescence can be observed in both living and dead individuals, which opens the possibility of sorting and using the same individuals in other experiments. The synthesis of Cdots is in constant development, and as new colours are designed, it will be possible to make even more complex studies.

On the other hand, other Cdots that accumulate in different parts of entomopathogenic nematodes, other than lysosomes, can also be tested, which could be a differentiating element for including more nematodes in the experiments. In conclusion, these results demonstrate that, in the future, ecological studies can be expanded to other small organisms in complex matrices like soil, in an economical and reliable manner, and with the possibility of accurately labelling hundreds of thousands of them.
